title: Mixing Vector Model for Copolymer Inference via Mixed Integer Linear Programming
summary: A novel two-phase molecule inference framework, mol-infer, has recently been developed to infer chemical graphs with prescribed abstract structures and desired property values through mixed integer linear programming (MILP) under the two-layered model, with guaranteed optimality and exactness relative to the given learned prediction function and structural constraints. In this study, we extend this framework to copolymers by introducing a simple feature representation, called the mixing vector (MV) model. In the proposed model, a copolymer feature vector is represented as a convex combination of MILP-tractable monomer descriptors weighted by the mixing ratio of the constituent monomers. This representation does not require explicit sequence-class information and is therefore naturally compatible with MILP-based inverse design. Under this model, we construct prediction functions for several copolymer property datasets using artificial neural networks, reduced quadratic multiple linear regression, and random forests. The proposed representation achieves practically useful predictive performance across multiple physicochemical property datasets; in particular, the best test R^2 score exceeds 0.7 for nine of the ten datasets and exceeds 0.9 for six datasets. We also formulate a multi-monomer inverse-design problem under the MV representation with a prescribed mixing ratio and show that the resulting MILP instances remain tractable, even for three-monomer settings. Finally, we perform an external consistency check by re-evaluating the inferred candidates and comparing the re-computed property values with those predicted by the learned model. Overall, the proposed framework gives a tractable first step toward model-level exact inverse design of copolymers under the two-layered model.

AI.tldr: This paper extends the mol-infer framework to copolymers using a mixing vector (MV) model, enabling MILP-based inverse design with good predictive performance across multiple datasets.
AI.motivation: To extend the existing two-phase molecule inference framework (mol-infer) from small molecules to copolymers, enabling exact inverse design of copolymer structures with desired properties.
AI.method: Introduces the mixing vector (MV) model, where copolymer feature vectors are convex combinations of monomer descriptors weighted by mixing ratios. Uses ANN, reduced quadratic multiple linear regression, and random forests for prediction. Formulates multi-monomer inverse-design as MILP.
AI.result: Achieves test R² > 0.7 for 9/10 datasets and > 0.9 for 6 datasets. The MILP instances remain tractable even for three-monomer settings. External consistency check validates the framework.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework provides a tractable first step toward model-level exact inverse design of copolymers under the two-layered model.

title: FPLIER: Federated Pathway-Level Information Extractor
comment: Accepted for publication at the ACM BCB '26 conference
summary: In transcriptomics, gene-set-aware factorization methods such as the Pathway Level Information Extractor (PLIER) are most effective when trained on large, heterogeneous expression compendia. Yet, many clinically relevant cohorts cannot be pooled into a single dataset due to privacy and governance constraints. We present FPLIER, a federated extension of PLIER that enables distributed training across multiple data holders while incorporating publicly available datasets. Through secure aggregation, FPLIER produces training updates algebraically equivalent to those of a centralized pooled-data approach while keeping expression data local. We evaluate FPLIER across multiple scenarios in two simulated consortia (from the K-CLIER and MultiPLIER studies) and demonstrate stable convergence. We further conduct a systematic analysis of membership inference attacks targeting both intermediate training statistics and the released model. Our results show that privacy risk is governed by the rank of the training expression matrix. Incorporating public data or reducing data dimensionality increases this rank, moving the system toward a full-rank regime in which training and non-training samples become indistinguishable to the attacker, and membership-inference performance approaches random guessing.

AI.tldr: FPLIER extends PLIER to a federated setting, enabling privacy-preserving, algebraically equivalent distributed transcriptomics training across multiple data holders while demonstrating that membership inference risk drops to random guessing when data dimensionality is reduced or public data is incorporated.
AI.motivation: PLIER is effective for gene-set-aware factorization on large expression compendia, but many clinically relevant datasets cannot be pooled due to privacy and governance constraints. FPLIER addresses this by enabling distributed training across data holders.
AI.method: FPLIER is a federated extension of PLIER using secure aggregation to produce training updates algebraically equivalent to centralized pooled training, while keeping expression data local.
AI.result: FPLIER achieves stable convergence across simulated consortia (K-CLIER, MultiPLIER). Privacy risk is governed by the rank of the training expression matrix; incorporating public data or reducing dimensionality increases rank, making membership inference attacks perform near random guessing.
AI.conclusion: FPLIER provides a privacy-preserving federated alternative to centralized PLIER training, with analytical guarantees on membership inference risk that approach random guessing under full-rank conditions.

title: Phylogenetic dynamics of MRCA ages and empirical moments of a Brownian trait
summary:
We study the temporal dynamics of the first two empirical moments of Brownian traits on phylogenetic trees. For a fixed tree, we characterize the distributions of their empirical mean and empirical variance across all lineages extant at any given time. In particular, we show that the variance of the empirical mean and the expected empirical variance are piecewise linear between diversification events.
  For lineage-homogeneous random trees, both the variance of the empirical mean and the expected empirical variance can be expressed in terms of the expected age of the most recent common ancestor (MRCA) of a uniformly sampled pair of extant lineages. In this representation, the expected MRCA age enters the two quantities with opposite signs, pointing to a structural opposition between the variance of the empirical mean and the expected empirical variance.
  For generalized birth-death processes with time-dependent speciation and extinction rates, we derive an explicit formula for the distribution of the MRCA age of a uniformly sampled pair of extant lineages. This yields integral expressions, at any time, for both the variance of the empirical mean and the expected empirical variance. In the constant-rate birth-death case, we further obtain closed-form expressions for the expected empirical variance and describe its asymptotic behavior in the supercritical, critical and subcritical regimes.

AI.tldr: This paper analyzes the temporal dynamics of empirical mean and variance of Brownian traits on phylogenetic trees, showing piecewise linear behavior and connecting these quantities to the expected MRCA age of extant lineages, with explicit formulas for birth-death processes.
AI.motivation: To understand how the first two empirical moments (mean and variance) of continuously evolving traits change over time on phylogenetic trees, and to characterize their distributional properties under different tree generation models.
AI.method: The authors analytically characterize distributions of empirical mean and variance on fixed trees, derive relationships with MRCA age for random trees, and obtain explicit formulas for generalized birth-death processes with time-dependent rates, including closed-form solutions for the constant-rate case.
AI.result: Key findings: (1) Variance of empirical mean and expected empirical variance are piecewise linear between diversification events on fixed trees; (2) For random trees, these quantities are expressed via expected MRCA age with opposite signs; (3) Explicit formulas derived for birth-death processes with asymptotic behavior characterized in supercritical, critical, and subcritical regimes.
AI.conclusion: The expected MRCA age serves as a unifying quantity linking the variance of empirical mean and expected empirical variance in opposition. The results provide a mathematical framework for understanding trait moment dynamics on phylogenies, with closed-form expressions available under constant-rate birth-death models.

title: Stochastic network epidemic model and particle filter: General framework and application to influenza in Japan
summary: Parameter inference and state estimation in stochastic and partially observed biological systems remain major problems in mathematical biology. In this work, we introduce a two-dimensional lattice graph model for the spread of infectious diseases. Estimating states and parameters in graph-based stochastic epidemic systems is particularly challenging because of randomness and incomplete observations. To address these issues, we propose a particle filter based data assimilation framework for the sequential estimation of both model states and unknown parameters. Two methodologies are developed: one based on the number of infected agents and another based on partial spatial location's information of infected agents on a two-dimensional lattice. The performance of the two methods are firstly analyzed and validated using synthetic data, and the first method is then applied to influenza data collected from different prefectures in Japan between July 2024 and December 2025. One-week-ahead forecasting simulations are also performed using current weekly data. The findings highlight the effectiveness of the proposed PF framework for real-time epidemic monitoring, forecasting, and adaptive public health decision-making.

AI.tldr: A particle filter framework is developed for estimating states and parameters in stochastic epidemic spread on 2D lattice graphs, using two methods (infection count-based and spatial location-based), validated on synthetic data and applied to Japanese influenza data (2024-2025) with one-week-ahead forecasting.
AI.motivation: Parameter inference and state estimation in stochastic partially observed biological systems (especially graph-based epidemic models) remain challenging due to randomness and incomplete observations.
AI.method: A particle filter based data assimilation framework on a two-dimensional lattice graph model, with two methodologies: one using the number of infected agents, another using partial spatial location information of infected agents.
AI.result: Both methods were validated using synthetic data; the first method was applied to real influenza data from Japanese prefectures (July 2024 - December 2025), with one-week-ahead forecasting performed using current weekly data.
AI.conclusion: The proposed PF framework is effective for real-time epidemic monitoring, forecasting, and adaptive public health decision-making.

title: Lattice Brownian bees with cooperative reproduction: steady states, collapse, and spreading
summary: We extend the ``Brownian bees'' model of Berestycki et al. (2021, 2022) to cooperative reproduction, $kA\to(k{+}1)A$, of a population of $N$ symmetric random walkers with removal, at each birth event, of the particle farthest from the origin. Working in the limit $N\to\infty$, we formulate a hydrodynamic free-boundary problem for this model. Using this formalism, we determine steady state population densities for all~$k$ and prove their linear stability for $k\le 2$ and instability for $k\ge 4$. In the marginal case $k=3$, there is a whole continuous family of steady states at a single, critical ratio of the reproduction and diffusion rates. Above criticality the population undergoes an asymptotically self-similar finite-time collapse to the origin. Below the criticality the population spreads diffusively, but the reproduction remains quantitatively relevant. For $k\ge 4$, the unstable steady state separates regimes of a finite-time collapse and a diffusive spreading. Here the collapse dynamics is asymptotically self-similar, and the population density exhibits a scale separation requiring a matched-asymptotic description. Our analytical predictions are confirmed by numerical solutions of the hydrodynamic free-boundary problem and by Monte Carlo simulations of the original microscopic model.

AI.tldr: This paper extends the Brownian bees model to cooperative reproduction ($kA \to (k+1)A$) with far-particle removal, deriving a hydrodynamic free-boundary problem. It finds steady states for all $k$, proves linear stability for $k\le 2$ and instability for $k\ge 4$, with $k=3$ as a marginal case exhibiting a continuous family of steady states. Above criticality the population collapses in finite time; below it spreads diffusively.
AI.motivation: To understand how cooperative reproduction (multiple particles cooperating to produce offspring) affects the spatial structure and critical behavior of a Brownian branching-and-removal system, generalizing prior work on the $k=1$ (pairwise branching) Brownian bees model.
AI.method: Hydrodynamic free-boundary problem formulation in the $N\to\infty$ limit, linear stability analysis, matched-asymptotic expansion for collapse dynamics, validated by numerical PDE solutions and Monte Carlo simulations of the microscopic model.
AI.result: Steady state densities derived for all $k$; linear stability for $k\le 2$, instability for $k\ge 4$; $k=3$ is marginal with a continuous family of steady states at a critical ratio. Above criticality: finite-time self-similar collapse; below criticality: diffusive spreading with quantitative reproduction effects. For $k\ge 4$, the unstable steady state separates collapse and spreading regimes.
AI.conclusion: Cooperative reproduction introduces richer phase behavior and critical transitions (marginal $k=3$, unstable $k\ge 4$) not present in the original pairwise branching model, with analytical predictions confirmed by numerics and simulations.

title: Training Ecosystems: A Computational Approach to Uncovering Learning Behavior in Unconventional Contexts
summary: Recent progress in diverse intelligence has shown simple learning capacities below the organism level - single cells and even molecular networks. However, there are still many knowledge gaps around learning capacity above the organism level, and about memory implemented purely by dynamical interactions without explicit memory media. We demonstrate that minimal ecological dynamics (in silico) are sufficient for several kinds of learning, assayed as changes in both, magnitude of response, and of recovery time. Systematic exploration of over 220,000 parameter combinations in a simulated classic predator-prey model revealed that, when perturbed by stimuli, recovery time exhibits habituation, sensitization, and a form of discrete number learning in a scale-invariant manner. Robustness analysis revealed that habituation and sensitization persist under stochastic perturbations, while discrete number learning is disrupted even at low noise levels. Dimensionality reduction revealed that the incidence of learning capacity is primarily determined by ecological interaction strengths. Clear, unique clustering patterns in parameter space allow high prediction accuracy for novel parameter combinations that enable learning. Response magnitude revealed a striking asymmetry: 90.6% of parameter combinations exhibited recovery time sensitization paired with habituation of response magnitude, while the opposite pattern was extremely rare. These findings highlight a set of phenomena at the intersection of ecology, basal cognition, and mathematics with many implications for a wide range of systems describable by similar kinds of equations. These properties provide numerous efforts in biology and engineering with a substrate that has considerable, pre-patterned, propensity for learning, which ultimately arises from mathematics, not depending on the details of physics or biology.

AI.tldr: This paper shows that simple predator-prey ecological dynamics can produce learning behaviors (habituation, sensitization, and number learning) without any memory substrate, purely through dynamical interactions, with learning capacity primarily determined by ecological interaction strengths.
AI.motivation: There are knowledge gaps about learning capacity above the organism level (ecological/collective systems) and about memory implemented purely by dynamical interactions without explicit memory media.
AI.method: Systematic exploration of over 220,000 parameter combinations in a simulated classic predator-prey model, perturbed by stimuli to measure changes in response magnitude and recovery time, with robustness analysis and dimensionality reduction.
AI.result: Recovery time exhibits habituation, sensitization, and scale-invariant discrete number learning. Habituation/sensitization persist under noise; number learning is disrupted by noise. 90.6% of parameter combinations show recovery time sensitization paired with habituation of response magnitude. Learning capacity is determined by ecological interaction strengths.
AI.conclusion: Learning capacity arises from mathematics itself, not dependent on specific physical or biological details, with implications for ecology, basal cognition, and engineering systems describable by similar equations.

title: Large language models reorganize representational geometry during in-context learning
summary: Large language models (LLMs) exhibit remarkable flexibility: they can adapt to novel tasks from in-context examples without any parameter updates, a capability known as in-context learning (ICL). Prior work on synthetic tasks has shown that ICL can implement specific algorithms, demonstrating architectural competence, and mechanistic analyses have identified key circuits that support this behavior. However, because in-context computation -- regardless of its algorithmic form -- relies on transformations in high-dimensional representation space, it remains unclear how the geometry of that space shapes ICL effectiveness. Motivated by the neuroscience view of classification as the untangling of neural representations, we hypothesize that ICL depends on the successful online untangling of task-relevant representations. To test this idea, we study how LLMs classify in-context examples whose labels are defined by the model's own internal representations with known structure. We show that ICL performance correlates systematically with the representational structure of the underlying classification task and that successful ICL is accompanied by geometric reorganization that increases online separability. We further find that LLM behavior is well described by a prototype-like algorithm that integrates evidence while reshaping representations to support classification. These findings offer a geometric account of ICL in pretrained LLMs, establish representational geometry as a mechanistic constraint on ICL, and quantify the gap between what pretrained representations afford and what in-context learning can exploit.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates how the geometric structure of internal representations in LLMs shapes in-context learning (ICL), showing that ICL effectiveness depends on the online untangling of task-relevant representations, and that ICL behavior follows a prototype-like algorithm that reorganizes representations to improve separability.
AI.motivation: To understand why ICL works in LLMs, the authors aim to bridge the gap between mechanistic circuit-level analyses and high-dimensional representational geometry, proposing that ICL effectiveness is constrained by how well task-relevant representations can be geometrically untangled in representation space.
AI.method: The authors study LLMs classifying in-context examples where labels are defined by the model's own internal representations with known structure. They correlate ICL performance with representational structure, analyze geometric reorganization during ICL, and test whether LLM behavior aligns with a prototype-like algorithm.
AI.result: ICL performance systematically correlates with the representational structure of the classification task; successful ICL is accompanied by geometric reorganization that increases online separability; LLM behavior is well described by a prototype-like algorithm.
AI.conclusion: The findings provide a geometric account of ICL in pretrained LLMs, establish representational geometry as a mechanistic constraint on ICL, and quantify the gap between what pretrained representations afford and what ICL can exploit.

title: Molecular Lead Optimization via Agentic Tool Planning
summary: Drug discovery is a lengthy and resource-intensive process composed of multiple stages. Among these stages, lead optimization plays a critical role in transforming early hit compounds into viable drug candidates. This stage requires improving ADMET-related properties through subtle structural refinement while preserving key molecular substructures responsible for binding affinity to disease targets. Recent advances in artificial intelligence have shown promise in accelerating various aspects of drug discovery; however, most existing approaches to lead optimization rely on one-step molecular optimization, which fail to account for the long-term consequences of sequential design decisions. To address this limitation, we propose TRACE, a trajectory-aware, LLM-reasoning agent for molecular lead optimization that formulates tool selection as a sequential decision-making problem over action trajectories. Given a lead molecule and an optimization objective, TRACE makes trajectory-aware decisions over molecular optimization tools, enabling forward-looking refinement under structural constraints. Experiments on multiple ADMET optimization tasks show that our agent achieves higher optimization success, larger property improvements, and higher validity, while preserving molecular similarity compared to baseline models.

AI.tldr: TRACE is a trajectory-aware, LLM-reasoning agent for molecular lead optimization that treats tool selection as a sequential decision-making problem, enabling forward-looking molecular refinement under structural constraints, and outperforms baselines on ADMET optimization tasks.
AI.motivation: Existing AI approaches for lead optimization rely on one-step molecular optimization, failing to account for the long-term consequences of sequential design decisions in drug discovery.
AI.method: TRACE formulates tool selection as a sequential decision-making problem over action trajectories, using LLM reasoning to make trajectory-aware decisions for molecular optimization under structural constraints.
AI.result: TRACE achieves higher optimization success, larger property improvements, and higher validity while preserving molecular similarity compared to baseline models on multiple ADMET optimization tasks.
AI.conclusion: Trajectory-aware sequential decision-making with LLM reasoning is effective for lead optimization in drug discovery, outperforming one-step approaches.

title: PROTOCOL: Late Interaction Retrieval for Protein Homolog Search
summary: Protein homology search underlies function annotation, structure prediction, and evolutionary analysis, but remains challenging in the "twilight zone," where global sequence similarity is weak and classical alignment methods lose sensitivity. Protein language models provide context-aware representations that could improve alignment sensitivity in this regime. However, prior protein embedding-based retrieval pipelines often pool these representations into a single vector, potentially obscuring local motifs, domains, or conserved residues that reveal remote homology. We introduce ProtoCol, a model which represents proteins as sets of residue embeddings and uses ColBERT-style late interaction to test whether residue-level comparison improves homolog retrieval. ProtoCol encodes proteins independently, keeps candidate representations pre-computable, and scores candidates with MaxSim over residue embeddings. On SCOPe superfamily and Pfam clan benchmarks, ProtoCol outperforms sequence-composition, alignment-based, pooled PLM, and trained single-vector baselines, supporting late interaction as an effective retrieval layer for remote homology search.

AI.tldr: ProtoCol uses late interaction over residue-level embeddings from protein language models to improve remote homology detection in the twilight zone, outperforming traditional alignment and pooled embedding methods.
AI.motivation: Protein homology search in the "twilight zone" where global sequence similarity is weak remains challenging; prior embedding-based methods pool representations into single vectors, potentially losing local motif and domain information critical for detecting remote homology.
AI.method: ProtoCol represents proteins as sets of residue embeddings from PLMs, uses ColBERT-style late interaction (MaxSim) for scoring, keeps candidate representations pre-computable, and performs residue-level comparison rather than single-vector pooling.
AI.result: On SCOPe superfamily and Pfam clan benchmarks, ProtoCol outperforms sequence-composition, alignment-based, pooled PLM, and trained single-vector baselines.
AI.conclusion: Late interaction is an effective retrieval layer for remote homology search, preserving local residue-level information while maintaining computational efficiency through pre-computation.

title: Traditional machine learning vs. deep learning from dynamic graph representations of proteins' 3D folds in the task of protein structure classification
comment: Main paper: 16 pages, 4 figures, and 1 table; Supplementary information: 13 pages, 9 figures
summary: Protein structure classification (PSC) uses supervised learning to predict a protein's CATH/SCOP(e) class from the protein's sequence or 3D structural feature(s). We already modeled 3D structures as (static) protein structure networks (PSNs), demonstrating the competitiveness of PSN-based features to sequence or direct (i.e. non-network) 3D structural features in the PSC task. More recently, we demonstrated the power of features extracted from dynamic PSNs over features extracted from static PSNs (and thus by transitivity over sequence and direct 3D structural features) in the same task. That dynamic PSN approach used traditional machine learning (ML), combining manual (pre-engineered) features with an off-the-shelf classifier. Here, we evaluate whether automatic deep learning (DL) from the dynamic PSNs yields improvements. Our evaluation on 72 datasets spanning ~44,000 CATH- or SCOPe-labeled dynamic PSNs reveals that in terms of PSC accuracy, traditional ML and DL are (close to) tied for a large majority of the datasets, while DL is on average 10+ times slower. We are the first to evaluate traditional ML vs. DL in the dynamic PSN-based PSC task.

AI.tldr: The paper compares traditional machine learning (ML) vs. deep learning (DL) for protein structure classification using dynamic protein structure networks (PSNs). Results show traditional ML matches DL in accuracy but is over 10x faster.
AI.motivation: To determine whether deep learning (DL) offers accuracy improvements over traditional machine learning (ML) when classifying protein structures using dynamic protein structure networks (PSNs).
AI.method: The authors compare traditional ML (manual features + off-the-shelf classifier) with automatic DL on dynamic PSNs. They evaluate both approaches across 72 datasets comprising ~44,000 CATH- or SCOPe-labeled dynamic PSNs.
AI.result: Traditional ML and DL achieve nearly identical accuracy on the majority of datasets, while DL is on average 10+ times slower than traditional ML.
AI.conclusion: Deep learning does not meaningfully outperform traditional machine learning in the dynamic PSN-based PSC task, and incurs a significant computational cost penalty. This is the first study to compare traditional ML vs. DL in this specific task.

title: Neural-Behavioral Representation of Natural Whole-body Movement in Monkeys
summary: Understanding how cortical activity represents natural whole-body behaviors in primates remains challenging. Limited by the diversity of movements and inaccessibility of large-scale neural representation of whole-body kinematics, previous motor decoding studies focused on constrained tasks and limited limb movements. Here, we present a neural-behavioral recording and modeling framework for freely moving monkeys, combining large-scale epidural cortical signals from distributed sensory- and motor-related areas with synchronized multi-view motion capture through a custom-made data collection platform. We reconstructed whole-body monkey kinematics and learned a compact behavior prior using an autoregressive encoder-decoder model. Conditioned on neural signals, the model decoded accurate and realistic whole-body movement without explicit physical constraints. Our results provide a novel proof-of-concept approach for decoding natural whole-body movements in primates using large-scale intracranial neural activity.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a neural-behavioral recording and modeling framework for freely moving monkeys, combining large-scale epidural cortical signals with multi-view motion capture to decode natural whole-body movements using an autoregressive encoder-decoder model.
AI.motivation: Understanding how cortical activity represents natural whole-body behaviors in primates is challenging due to movement diversity and limited access to large-scale neural representation of whole-body kinematics. Previous studies were constrained to limited tasks and limb movements.
AI.method: The authors developed a framework for freely moving monkeys combining large-scale epidural cortical signals from distributed sensory and motor areas with synchronized multi-view motion capture. They used an autoregressive encoder-decoder model to learn a compact behavior prior and decode whole-body kinematics from neural signals.
AI.result: The model decoded accurate and realistic whole-body movements without explicit physical constraints, providing a novel proof-of-concept for decoding natural whole-body movements in primates.
AI.conclusion: This work demonstrates that large-scale intracranial neural activity can effectively decode natural whole-body movements in freely moving primates, overcoming limitations of previous constrained-task approaches.

title: Common Noise-Induced Group-Level Synchronization Between Uncoupled Groups of Oscillators
summary: We investigate group-level synchronization between oscillator groups induced by common noise in the absence of inter-group coupling. Each group receives a common noise shared by all its oscillators and independent local noise inputs to individual oscillators. The same common noise is applied to all groups. The system is studied with both identical and nonidentical oscillators, and with and without intra-group coupling. In the nonidentical case, natural frequencies are drawn from the same distribution for both groups, making them statistically equivalent. Through numerical simulations of this system, we find that the degree of synchronization within each group, measured by the absolute value of a complex Kuramoto order parameter, typically shows significant temporal fluctuations. Importantly, the complex order parameters representing the collective oscillations of the groups synchronize when the groups are driven by the same common noise. By deriving a phase density evolution mapping, we analytically explain how this group-level synchronization is achieved in the absence of intra-group coupling.

AI.tldr: This paper studies how groups of oscillators driven by the same common noise can synchronize at the group level even without inter-group coupling, using Kuramoto order parameters and phase density evolution mapping.
AI.motivation: To understand whether and how common noise can induce group-level synchronization between distinct oscillator populations without direct coupling between the groups.
AI.method: Numerical simulations of oscillator groups with common and independent noise inputs, plus analytical derivation of a phase density evolution mapping to explain group-level synchronization without intra-group coupling.
AI.result: Complex order parameters representing collective oscillations of groups synchronize when driven by the same common noise, despite significant temporal fluctuations in within-group synchronization.
AI.conclusion: Common noise alone can induce group-level synchronization between oscillator groups, and this phenomenon is analytically explainable through phase density evolution mapping even in the absence of intra-group coupling.

title: Brain-IT-VQA: From Brain Signals to Answers
summary: Decoding visual content from fMRI signals recorded while a person views images, and specifically answering questions about the seen images, is a long-standing challenge. While significant progress has been made in recent years in visual question answering (VQA) from fMRI, performance remains limited. Moreover, although recent models can make increasingly accurate predictions, they have rarely been used as tools for understanding the structure of visual representations in the brain. We present Brain-IT-VQA, a framework for visual question answering from fMRI. Building on the Brain Interaction Transformer (Brain-IT), our method decodes language tokens from brain activity and integrates them with a language model to answer visual questions. Our model substantially outperforms previous fMRI-based captioning and VQA approaches. We further introduce NSD-VQA, a new dataset and benchmark for visual question answering from fMRI. Unlike existing image-fMRI VQA datasets, which typically provide only a few broad and weakly controlled questions per image, NSD-VQA provides on average 20 question-answer pairs per image across 20 controlled question categories that disentangle multiple levels of visual understanding. This enables more reliable and interpretable evaluation despite limited fMRI test data. Together, Brain-IT-VQA and NSD-VQA provide both a strong predictive framework and a tool for studying brain representations. Using this benchmark, we quantify which forms of visual and semantic information can be reliably decoded from fMRI responses to natural images. We further analyze the contributions of different brain regions across question types.

AI.tldr: Brain-IT-VQA is a framework for visual question answering from fMRI that outperforms prior methods, and NSD-VQA is a new benchmark with controlled question categories enabling reliable evaluation and analysis of brain representations.
AI.motivation: To improve VQA from fMRI by developing a better predictive framework and a more controlled, interpretable benchmark, while using the model as a tool to study visual representations in the brain.
AI.method: Brain-IT-VQA builds on the Brain Interaction Transformer (Brain-IT) to decode language tokens from fMRI signals and integrate them with a language model for answering visual questions. NSD-VQA provides 20 Q&A pairs per image across 20 controlled question categories.
AI.result: The model substantially outperforms previous fMRI-based captioning and VQA approaches. The benchmark enables reliable evaluation and quantification of which visual and semantic information can be decoded from fMRI, along with analysis of brain region contributions.
AI.conclusion: Brain-IT-VQA and NSD-VQA together offer a strong predictive framework and a tool for studying brain representations, enabling analysis of decodable visual/semantic information and regional brain contributions across question types.

title: Embodied Virtual Reality Feedback Reshapes Neural Representations to Support Continuous Three-Dimensional Motor Imagery Decoding
comment: 28 pages, 7 figures, 3 tables. Submitted to Nature Biomedical Engineering. Data to be made available via Zenodo (DOI: 10.5281/zenodo.16047021)
summary: Continuous brain-computer interfaces (BCIs) that decode motion trajectories from imagined movement offer intuitive motor control, yet how feedback modality and longitudinal training shape neural representations and decoding performance remains poorly understood. We present the first systematic investigation of embodied virtual reality (VR) feedback during real-time 3D virtual limb control driven by motor imagery, across ten longitudinal sessions in ten participants. Performance was evaluated using three strategies: actual online performance (Fixed Decoder Generalisation, FDG), periodic retraining (Sequential Adaptive Training, SAT), and within-session upper-bound estimation (Within-Session Reconstruction, WSR). A CNN-LSTM decoder achieved within-session imagined movement correlations of r = 0.762 under VR and r = 0.672 under screen feedback. VR significantly outperformed screen feedback across all strategies and movement dimensions (improvements of 8.9-13.0%, all p <= 0.002, d = 1.42-2.05). This advantage persisted under fixed decoders without retraining, demonstrating that embodied VR feedback elicits inherently more decodable and generalisable neural representations. Linear mixed-effects modelling confirmed robust main effects of feedback modality and movement axis with no interaction. Neurophysiologically, VR produced stronger sensorimotor-parietal desynchronisation and enhanced motor-frontal functional connectivity, with pervasive anterior insula engagement across all frequency bands and increased superior parietal lobule coupling, paralleling patterns associated with real movement execution. These findings establish embodied spatial feedback as a key design principle for next-generation continuous BCIs targeting intuitive motor control and neurorehabilitation.

AI.tldr: This study shows that embodied VR feedback significantly outperforms screen feedback in continuous BCI decoding of imagined 3D limb movements across 10 participants and 10 sessions, with VR achieving higher decoding accuracy and more brain activity patterns resembling real movement execution.
AI.motivation: To investigate how feedback modality (embodied VR vs. screen) and longitudinal training affect neural representations and decoding performance in continuous BCIs for imagined movement, as this remains poorly understood.
AI.method: 10 participants performed 10 sessions of real-time 3D virtual limb control using motor imagery under VR and screen feedback. A CNN-LSTM decoder was used with three evaluation strategies: Fixed Decoder Generalisation (FDG), Sequential Adaptive Training (SAT), and Within-Session Reconstruction (WSR). Neurophysiological analysis included sensorimotor-parietal desynchronisation and functional connectivity.
AI.result: VR achieved r=0.762 vs. r=0.672 (screen) for within-session decoding. VR outperformed screen by 8.9-13.0% across all strategies (p<=0.002, d=1.42-2.05). VR produced stronger sensorimotor-parietal desynchronisation, enhanced motor-frontal connectivity, anterior insula engagement, and increased superior parietal lobule coupling.
AI.conclusion: Embodied spatial VR feedback is a key design principle for next-generation continuous BCIs targeting intuitive motor control and neurorehabilitation, as it elicits more decodable and generalisable neural representations that parallel real movement execution patterns.

title: Digitally enriching a screening population for pancreatic cancer using routine blood-based measures and clinical histories
summary: Earlier detection of pancreatic cancer is key to enabling wider access to curative treatment and reducing cancer deaths; however, screening is presently not viable. Latent indicators of pathology are evident in an individual's disease and blood test trajectories and may predict the development of pancreatic cancer. Longitudinal sequences of coded diagnoses and blood test values accrued by patients throughout their clinical interactions were used to train a custom Transformer-based neural network with a multi-head attention mechanism to predict risk of pancreatic cancer with a multi-year lead time and risk-stratify populations for targeted screening. The cohort comprised 6,017 adults with pancreatic cancer and 177,081 controls (overall median age 75, 45% female) with median 12 years (interquartile range 6.9-16.2) of medical history prior to pancreatic cancer diagnosis. External validation via leave-one-site-out, out-of-sample testing predicting pancreatic cancer 1-, 2-, and 3-years prior to diagnosis demonstrated mean area under the receiver operating characteristic of 0.837 (95% confidence interval 0.827-0.848), 0.797 (95% confidence interval 0.782-0.813), and 0.760 (95% confidence interval 0.745-0.776), respectively. Estimated pancreatic cancer risks were well-calibrated (calibration plot slope 1.08, intercept of -0.077; Brier score 0.025), and a Bayesian population pancreatic cancer prevalence update allows estimated cancer risk outputs to be transportable across settings. At testing, a screening threshold of >3.3% risk of pancreatic cancer in 1-year offered a diagnostic odds ratio of 18.2. Our work therefore lays the foundation for a first population-level digital enrichment tool to widen access to curative-intent management of pancreatic cancer.

AI.tldr: A Transformer-based neural network using longitudinal clinical data (diagnoses and blood tests) can predict pancreatic cancer risk up to 3 years before diagnosis with good accuracy (AUC 0.760-0.837), offering a potential digital screening tool for risk stratification.
AI.motivation: Earlier detection of pancreatic cancer is critical for curative treatment and reducing mortality, but current screening methods are not viable for population-level use.
AI.method: A custom Transformer neural network with multi-head attention was trained on longitudinal sequences of coded diagnoses and blood test values from 6,017 pancreatic cancer patients and 177,081 controls, with external validation via leave-one-site-out testing.
AI.result: Mean AUC of 0.837 (1-year), 0.797 (2-year), and 0.760 (3-year) prior to diagnosis; well-calibrated with calibration slope 1.08, Brier score 0.025; screening threshold >3.3% risk gave diagnostic odds ratio of 18.2.
AI.conclusion: This work provides a foundation for a population-level digital enrichment tool to enable targeted screening and widen access to curative-intent management of pancreatic cancer.
